test: Multiparametric MRI of the prostate scored with PI-RADS (v2 / v2.1)
reference_standard: Targeted biopsy (MRI-fusion or cognitive) +/- systematic biopsy, OR template-prostate-mapping (TPM) biopsy, OR radical-prostatectomy histology in cancer-positive patients
target_condition: Clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa) - typically Gleason >=3+4 / ISUP grade group >=2 (PROMIS used the higher cut-off Gleason >=4+3 / ISUP >=3 for its primary endpoint)
population: Men with clinical suspicion of prostate cancer (elevated PSA / abnormal DRE); biopsy-naive and prior-negative-biopsy subgroups; PI-RADS threshold typically >=3 considered positive

primary_tier.raw_quote: 740 men, 576 of whom underwent 1.5 Tesla MP-MRI followed by both TRUS-biopsy and TPM-biopsy. On TPM-biopsy, 408 (71%) of 576 men had cancer with 230 (40%) of 576 patients clinically significant. For clinically significant cancer, MP-MRI was more sensitive (93%, 95% CI 88-96%) than TRUS-biopsy (48%, 42-55%; p<0.0001) and less specific (41%, 36-46% for MP-MRI vs 96%, 94-98% for TRUS-biopsy; p<0.0001).

275 patients enrolled; 24 (9%) excluded; 251 analysed. 53 (21%) of 251 analysed patients had negative (Likert <=2) multiparametric MRI. csPCa-A (ISUP >=2) was detected in 94 (37%) of 251 patients. 13 (14%) of these 94 patients were diagnosed by systematic biopsy only [csPCa+ but MRI-]. csPCa-A would have been missed in 5.2% (95% CI 2.8-8.7) of patients had systematic biopsy not been done.

291 men underwent MRI, pelvic-only PSMA, and systematic +/- targeted biopsy ... csPCa was any International Society of Urological Pathology (ISUP) grade group >=2 malignancy. Of the patients, 56% (n=162) had csPCa; 67% had PI-RADS 3-5 ... Sensitivity also improved (97% vs 83%, p<0.001); however, specificity was reduced (40% vs 53%, p=0.011) [for combined PSMA+MRI vs MRI alone]. MRI-alone arm: Sensitivity 83%, Specificity 53%.

346 patients from center 1 and 292 patients from center 2 [N=638] ... Mp-MRI (TWI, DWI, and DCE) ... Pathologies at biopsy and/or prostatectomy specimens were used as standard references. Op-MRI had comparable sensitivity (senior/junior: 90.9% [261/287] / 91.6% [263/287]) and higher specificity (78.1% [274/351] / 74.4% [261/351]) compared with Mp-MRI (sensitivity: 92.3% [265/287] / 93.7% [269/287]; specificity: 67.8% [238/351] / 68.1% [239/351]).

primary_tier.country: International (Netherlands + Norway primary; readers across 20 countries)
primary_tier.raw_quote: 1000 testing cases ... reference standard established by histopathology and at least 3 years (median 5 [IQR 4-6] years) of follow-up. The AI system showed lower specificity (68.9% [95% CI 65.3-72.4] vs 69.0% [65.5-72.5]) at the same sensitivity (96.1%, 94.0-98.2) as the PI-RADS 3 or greater operating point [for radiology readings made during multidisciplinary practice]. Of 10,207 examinations, 2440 (23.9%) had Gleason grade group 2 or greater prostate cancer.

sensitivity_tier_added.raw_quote: 55 men with suspicion of PCa and negative systematic biopsies or clinically discordant low-risk PCa (n=21) or those being considered for FT (n=34) ... All suspicious lesions underwent PET/MR-ultrasound fusion biopsies. Overall, 61/114 lesions (53.5%) identified on any modality were malignant; 49/61 lesions (80.3%) were csPCa. On lesion-level analysis, for detection of csPCa, the sensitivity of PET was higher than that of mpMR and PET/MR (86% vs 67% and 69% [...]), but at a lower specificity (32% vs 85% and 86%, respectively).

_engine_expected_flags.note: These are documentation only - the engine recomputes at page-load
_engine_expected_flags.expected_fallback_combined: REML or FE bivariate (k=6 - REML may converge or fall back depending on heterogeneity)
_engine_expected_flags.expected_fallback_primary: REML or FE bivariate (k=5 - boundary case)
heterogeneity_axes: csPCa_definition (Gleason >=4+3 in PROMIS vs Gleason >=3+4 / ISUP >=2 elsewhere)
